name: Postpositions
summary: Turkish puts its 'prepositions' after the noun, and many of them demand a specific case.
explanation:
Where English uses prepositions before a noun (with me, for you, after lunch), Turkish uses postpositions that come after the noun. Benimle means 'with me', senin için means 'for you', and yemekten sonra means 'after lunch'. The position is the mirror image of English.

Many postpositions govern a particular case on the noun in front of them. Sonra (after) and önce (before) take the ablative -den, so okuldan sonra means 'after school'. Kadar (until, as much as) and için (for) usually follow a plain or genitive noun, while gibi (like) follows a plain noun: aslan gibi (like a lion).

The postposition ile (with) is special: it often shrinks into a suffix. Ile harmonises and attaches as -le or -la, with a buffer -y- after a vowel. Ali ile becomes Ali'yle, araba ile becomes arabayla, and ben ile becomes benimle. Learning which case each postposition wants is the main work here.
pattern: noun (+ required case) + postposition (sonra, önce, için, gibi, kadar, ile)

examples.en: We drink tea after the meal.
I had breakfast before the lesson.
He is strong like a lion.

commonMistakes: Placing the postposition before the noun like an English preposition, saying için sen instead of senin için.
Forgetting the case a postposition requires, saying yemek sonra instead of yemekten sonra.
Writing ile as a separate word when it should contract, saying araba ile instead of arabayla.
Dropping the buffer -y- in the contracted form, saying Ali'le instead of Ali'yle.
Using gibi with a case-marked noun instead of a plain one, saying aslanı gibi instead of aslan gibi.
tip: Read the phrase backwards from English: 'with me' is 'me + with' = benimle. Then check whether the postposition demands -den (sonra, önce) or a plain noun (gibi, için).
